title: TABI - Discover Japan
description: Your guide to the real Japan: travel, culture, food, hidden places, and the things worth bringing home.

items.title: Shibuya on the First Night: An Orientation Walk That Does Not Overreach
items.summary: Shibuya is useful on a first Tokyo evening because rail access, crossings, food streets, shopping, and hotels sit close together. This route keeps the night practical while still feeling like arrival in Tokyo.
items.content_text: Shibuya is useful on a first Tokyo evening because rail access, crossings, food streets, shopping, and hotels sit close together. This route keeps the night practical while still feeling like arrival in Tokyo.

items.title: Yokohama Minato Mirai for a First Easy Waterfront Day
items.summary: Minato Mirai gives the Tokyo area a useful first-day alternative: bay views, wide promenades, museums, shopping, and rail access without the same pressure as a dense central Tokyo route.
items.content_text: Minato Mirai gives the Tokyo area a useful first-day alternative: bay views, wide promenades, museums, shopping, and rail access without the same pressure as a dense central Tokyo route.

items.title: Kichijoji and Inokashira Park for a Slower Tokyo Day
items.summary: Kichijoji works when Tokyo needs air, shade, local shopping, and a park-centered pause. The day is strongest when Inokashira Park is the anchor, not just a quick photo stop.
items.content_text: Kichijoji works when Tokyo needs air, shade, local shopping, and a park-centered pause. The day is strongest when Inokashira Park is the anchor, not just a quick photo stop.

items.title: Nakanoshima, Osaka: A River and Architecture Walk Between Busy Districts
items.summary: Nakanoshima is useful because it gives Osaka a quieter civic and riverside layer between busier shopping and nightlife areas. The best route treats the island as a bridge, not a detour.
items.content_text: Nakanoshima is useful because it gives Osaka a quieter civic and riverside layer between busier shopping and nightlife areas. The best route treats the island as a bridge, not a detour.

items.title: Shinsekai for Kushikatsu and Retro Osaka Without Turning It Into a Cliche
items.summary: Shinsekai is easy to flatten into neon, snacks, and nostalgia. A better visit keeps the food simple, reads the neighborhood context, and checks current access before building the night around one shop.
items.content_text: Shinsekai is easy to flatten into neon, snacks, and nostalgia. A better visit keeps the food simple, reads the neighborhood context, and checks current access before building the night around one shop.

items.title: Tenma in the Evening: Small Plates, Counters, and a Better Osaka Night
items.summary: Tenma is useful when you want Osaka food energy with more small-counter rhythm than spectacle. Go slowly and order in rounds.
items.content_text: Tenma is useful when you want Osaka food energy with more small-counter rhythm than spectacle. Go slowly and order in rounds.

items.title: Tenugui Guide: Patterns, Uses, and How to Choose One as a Souvenir
items.summary: A tenugui is light, useful, and full of pattern language. Learn how to choose one by fabric, motif, season, and whether you want to use it, frame it, or gift it.
items.content_text: A tenugui is light, useful, and full of pattern language. Learn how to choose one by fabric, motif, season, and whether you want to use it, frame it, or gift it.

items.title: Tsuruhashi and Osaka Korea Town With Food Context
items.summary: Tsuruhashi and nearby Korea Town add another food and neighborhood layer to Osaka. Visit with curiosity and respect for living communities.
items.content_text: Tsuruhashi and nearby Korea Town add another food and neighborhood layer to Osaka. Visit with curiosity and respect for living communities.

items.title: Ekiben: How to Choose a Station Bento That Feels Like the Region
items.summary: Ekiben make train travel taste specific. Learn how to read the display, choose regional ingredients, and eat on board without turning lunch into a mess.
items.content_text: Ekiben make train travel taste specific. Learn how to read the display, choose regional ingredients, and eat on board without turning lunch into a mess.

items.title: Kuromon Market Without Overdoing It
items.summary: Kuromon Market can be fun, but it is easy to turn a food walk into expensive grazing. Go with a plan and leave room for a proper meal elsewhere.
items.content_text: Kuromon Market can be fun, but it is easy to turn a food walk into expensive grazing. Go with a plan and leave room for a proper meal elsewhere.

items.title: Namba and Dotonbori for a First Osaka Night That Still Works
items.summary: Namba and Dotonbori make Osaka?s food energy immediately visible. Use the area as a first-night introduction, not the whole story of Osaka.
items.content_text: Namba and Dotonbori make Osaka?s food energy immediately visible. Use the area as a first-night introduction, not the whole story of Osaka.

items.title: Nakazakicho for Cafes, Small Shops, and a Slower Osaka Afternoon
items.summary: Nakazakicho gives Osaka a quieter texture close to Umeda: cafes, small shops, older lanes, and enough room to wander.
items.content_text: Nakazakicho gives Osaka a quieter texture close to Umeda: cafes, small shops, older lanes, and enough room to wander.

items.title: Kumano Kodo Villages Without Rushing the Pilgrimage
items.summary: The Kumano Kodo is not only a trail; it is a chain of villages, baths, forest paths, and small meals. Slow the route down and the pilgrimage becomes easier to feel.
items.content_text: The Kumano Kodo is not only a trail; it is a chain of villages, baths, forest paths, and small meals. Slow the route down and the pilgrimage becomes easier to feel.

items.title: Yanaka, Nezu, and Sendagi for a Slower Tokyo Walk
items.summary: The Yanesen area shows Tokyo at a smaller scale: lanes, temples, old shops, and residential rhythm. It is best treated as a walk, not a checklist.
items.content_text: The Yanesen area shows Tokyo at a smaller scale: lanes, temples, old shops, and residential rhythm. It is best treated as a walk, not a checklist.

items.title: Quiet Coastal Towns for a Slower Japan Trip
items.summary: Japan?s coast is full of small towns where seafood, ferries, morning markets, and sea walls set the pace. This guide helps you add one coastal pause without overcomplicating the route.
items.content_text: Japan?s coast is full of small towns where seafood, ferries, morning markets, and sea walls set the pace. This guide helps you add one coastal pause without overcomplicating the route.

items.title: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa for Coffee, Gardens, and a Softer Tokyo Morning
items.summary: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa works as a slower Tokyo morning because cafes, waterside streets, and garden time can fit into one compact route.
items.content_text: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa works as a slower Tokyo morning because cafes, waterside streets, and garden time can fit into one compact route.

items.title: Sakai for Knives, Craft, and an Old Osaka Day Trip
items.summary: Sakai is one of the best Osaka-area day trips for travelers interested in craft, especially knives, old streets, and a different city rhythm.
items.content_text: Sakai is one of the best Osaka-area day trips for travelers interested in craft, especially knives, old streets, and a different city rhythm.

items.title: Shimokitazawa and Koenji for Vintage Shops and Small Music Energy
items.summary: Shimokitazawa and Koenji are useful for Tokyo small-shop culture without a department-store day. Browse slowly and avoid turning secondhand shopping into a haul.
items.content_text: Shimokitazawa and Koenji are useful for Tokyo small-shop culture without a department-store day. Browse slowly and avoid turning secondhand shopping into a haul.

items.title: Ginza and Nihonbashi for Design, Food Halls, and Calm Shopping
items.summary: Ginza and Nihonbashi can be a compact design and food-hall walk, not only a luxury shopping day. Use them for quality browsing, not pressure buying.
items.content_text: Ginza and Nihonbashi can be a compact design and food-hall walk, not only a luxury shopping day. Use them for quality browsing, not pressure buying.

items.title: Japanese Stationery Worth Bringing Home, From Notebooks to Pens
items.summary: Japan is a stationery country if you know where to look. Choose notebooks, pens, tapes, and desk tools that are useful after the trip, not just beautiful in the shop.
items.content_text: Japan is a stationery country if you know where to look. Choose notebooks, pens, tapes, and desk tools that are useful after the trip, not just beautiful in the shop.
